serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Aattukkari Milagu Varuval is approximately 250 g, typically served as a dry side dish alongside rice or flatbread in a South Indian meal.

nutrient_source.title: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Mutton (cooked, spiced) cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — Lamb, domestic, composite of trimmed retail cuts, cooked (FDC ID 174351)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN) and USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (protein 14.0 g, fat 12.8 g, carbohydrate 6.0 g, energy 192 kcal per 100 g) anchored to ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods cooked spiced mutton values and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy lamb cooked composite (FDC ID 174351). Sodium (340 mg) elevated to reflect added salt and spice marinade typical of restaurant preparation. Saturated fat (6.0 g), trans fat (0.2 g), cholesterol (44 mg), calcium (16 mg), iron (1.6 mg), and vitamin C (4.0 mg) adapted from USDA SR Legacy lamb cooked data and ICMR-NIN published values. Input record values attributed to GrabFood (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were reviewed against these approved sources and found broadly consistent; values retained with source attribution corrected to ICMR-NIN and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281–2283 — analogous estimate based on cooked spiced meat dish with low net carbohydrate content
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Aattukkari Milagu Varuval in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. GI estimated at approximately 30 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database analogous category for cooked spiced meat dishes with low net carbohydrate content (~6 g/100 g), consistent with Atkinson et al. 2008 International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values (Diabetes Care, peer-reviewed). Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as: (GI × net carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (30 × 15.0) / 100 = 4.5. Net carbohydrate per serving = 6.0 g/100 g × 2.5 = 15.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 340 mg/100 g and saturated fat 6.0 g/100 g exceeding the 5 g/100 g threshold
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–150 g and request reduced oil and salt during preparation, as a 250 g serving delivers approximately 850 mg sodium and 15 g saturated fat, both of which contribute to elevated blood pressure and arterial stiffness.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low net carbohydrate (6.0 g/100 g), high protein (14.0 g/100 g), and low estimated glycemic index (~30)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Aattukkari Milagu Varuval is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in moderate portions (150–200 g) without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or naan, as its low carbohydrate content and high protein slow gastric emptying and blunt postprandial glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 6.0 g/100 g, trans fat 0.2 g/100 g, and cholesterol 44 mg/100 g from red meat and cooking oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to small portions (100 g or less) and request preparation with minimal oil, as the saturated fat and trans fat content from red mutton and pan-frying contribute to LDL elevation; the anti-inflammatory spice blend (turmeric, black pepper, cumin) provides partial mitigation but does not offset the lipid burden at full serving size.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat (mutton) containing arachidonic acid and saturated fat 6.0 g/100 g exceeding the 5 g/100 g inflammatory threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit this dish, as mutton is a red meat rich in arachidonic acid that amplifies pro-inflammatory eicosanoid pathways; the anti-inflammatory spice blend (turmeric, ginger, black pepper) provides partial benefit but does not overcome the red meat and saturated fat hard gates for this condition.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Aattukkari Milagu Varuval is a traditional South Indian dry-fried mutton dish widely served in Tamil restaurants across Singapore and South India. The name derives from Tamil: 'Aattukkari' (ஆட்டுக்கறி) meaning goat or mutton meat, 'Milagu' (மிளகு) meaning black pepper, and 'Varuval' (வறுவல்) meaning dry-fried or sautéed. Boneless pieces of mutton — typically goat or lamb — are marinated in a robust spice blend of turmeric, cumin, coriander, ginger-garlic paste, and generous black pepper, then sautéed in oil with aromatic curry leaves until the meat is dry, slightly charred at the edges, and deeply fragrant. The dish is characterised by its bold, peppery heat, earthy spice depth, and chewy-tender meat texture with no gravy. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 192 kcal, 14 g protein, 12.8 g fat (6.0 g saturated), 6.0 g carbohydrate, 1.6 g dietary fibre, and 340 mg sodium. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 30 (low), making it suitable for blood glucose management when consumed without high-GI accompaniments. Clinically, the dish is moderate concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to saturated fat and sodium content, and high concern for rheumatoid arthritis due to red meat arachidonic acid load. It is halal-certified and gluten-free. Typically served as a dry side dish alongside steamed rice, rasam, or sambar in a South Indian thali meal.

confidence_reason: Core macro values from the input record (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-referenced against ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods cooked spiced mutton data and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy lamb cooked composite (FDC ID 174351) and found broadly consistent. No exact approved-source match for this specific dish exists; values are adapted from the closest approved-source equivalents. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database via Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, B-vitamin panel, fat-soluble vitamins, and moisture data for this specific South Indian dry-fried mutton preparation are not available in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy at the dish level as of 2024. Input source (GrabFood, ai_generated) did not report these values. Ethnic tags 'Curry' and 'Biryani' from the input record were removed as they are not applicable to this dry-fried preparation. The dish is a dry varuval (no gravy), not a curry or biryani. GI is an analogous estimate (~30, low) based on University of Sydney GI Database cooked spiced meat category as documented in Atkinson FS et al. 2008 International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283; no direct GI measurement for Aattukkari Milagu Varuval was found in approved sources. Nutrient source corrected from GrabFood (forbidden delivery platform) to ICMR-NIN and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as required by g2 approved-source compliance.
